food_name: Bun Thit Nuong (Vietnamese Grilled Pork Vermicelli Bowl)
food_subgroup: Vietnamese room-temperature rice vermicelli bowl with grilled pork, fresh herbs, pickled vegetables, crushed peanuts, and nuoc cham dressing

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn URI exists for Bun Thit Nuong. Closest parent would be a generic noodle dish concept. Null retained pending FoodOn expansion for Southeast Asian dishes.
ontology.foodex2_description: Vietnamese vermicelli noodle bowl — closest parent match in FoodEx2 hierarchy for rice-noodle-based mixed dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0CMF is the closest defensible FoodEx2 code for a Vietnamese rice vermicelli mixed bowl. No exact code exists for Bun Thit Nuong specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Bun Thit Nuong or Vietnamese vermicelli bowl as a specific food concept.

preparation_method: boiled rice vermicelli served at room temperature
main_ingredients: nuoc cham (fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, chilli, garlic)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately one bowl weighing 450g, which includes the rice vermicelli base, grilled pork, fresh herbs, pickled vegetables, crushed peanuts, and approximately 50ml of nuoc cham dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia — Bun Thit Nuong entry
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table 2020, which includes Vietnamese composite dishes. Values represent the full assembled bowl including nuoc cham at the stated 450g portion, normalized to per-100g basis. FAO/INFOODS SEA is an approved primary regional source for Southeast Asian dishes under Project Veritas source policy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — rice vermicelli (bún) GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 58 is based on rice vermicelli (thin white rice noodle, bún) which has a published GI range of 58–64 in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). The room-temperature serving method and presence of pickled vegetables (acidity) provide modest GI moderation. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (58 × 58g) / 100 = 33.6. Carbohydrate per 450g serving = 12.9 × 4.5 = 58.05g ≈ 58g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content ~850mg per bowl (189mg/100g × 450g), primarily from nuoc cham dipping sauce (~500mg of total sodium)
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should limit nuoc cham to 25ml or less per serving, which reduces total sodium to approximately 600mg per bowl — a more acceptable intake compared to broth-based Vietnamese soups such as Pho Bo (~1,100mg) or Bun Bo Hue (~1,650mg); the absence of sodium-dense broth makes this one of the lower-sodium Vietnamese noodle options.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycemic load of 33.6 per bowl driven by rice vermicelli base (GI ~58); pickled vegetable acidity provides modest GI attenuation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request a smaller vermicelli portion (reducing GL to approximately 20–25), retain the pickled daikon and carrot (acidity blunts postprandial glucose rise), use nuoc cham sparingly to limit added sugar, and note that Bun Thit Nuong has a lower GL than Com Tam (~GL 46.8) or Bun Bo Hue, making it a relatively better glycaemic choice among Vietnamese noodle dishes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 1.3g/100g (5.85g per bowl); sodium 850mg per bowl; charcoal-grilled pork generates heterocyclic amines (HCAs) at moderate levels
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, Bun Thit Nuong is acceptable in moderation — saturated fat per bowl is relatively low at ~5.9g, the fresh herb mix (mint, perilla) contributes anti-inflammatory polyphenols including rosmarinic acid, and crushed peanuts provide MUFA; however, sodium should be managed by limiting nuoc cham, and charcoal-grilled preparations should be consumed occasionally rather than daily to minimise HCA exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Fresh herb mix (mint, perilla, Vietnamese coriander) provides anti-inflammatory polyphenols; crushed peanuts add MUFA
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Bun Thit Nuong is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients — the fresh herbs (mint with menthol, perilla with rosmarinic acid, Vietnamese coriander with quercetin) provide Grade C anti-inflammatory support, and the dish is free of dairy and gluten; charcoal-grilled pork generates some HCAs but at lower levels than heavily charred preparations.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bun Thit Nuong (Bún Thịt Nướng) is a classic Southern Vietnamese room-temperature vermicelli bowl distinguished by the complete absence of broth. The dish consists of thin white rice noodles (bún) served at ambient temperature, topped with charcoal-grilled lemongrass-marinated pork, a generous mix of fresh herbs including mint, perilla, and Vietnamese coriander, pickled carrot and daikon, and crushed roasted peanuts. The entire bowl is dressed with nuoc cham — a balanced Vietnamese dipping sauce of fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, chilli, and garlic. Per standard bowl (~450g including 50ml nuoc cham): approximately 510 kcal, 22g protein, 16g fat, 58g carbohydrate, GI ~58, GL ~33.6, and ~850mg sodium. The pickled vegetables' acidity modestly attenuates the postprandial glycaemic response. The fresh herb mix contributes anti-inflammatory polyphenols (rosmarinic acid from perilla, quercetin from Vietnamese coriander). Compared to hot Vietnamese noodle soups, Bun Thit Nuong is one of the lower-sodium options due to the absence of sodium-dense broth. Nuoc cham accounts for approximately 500mg of the total sodium. Clinically, this dish is a better glycaemic choice than Com Tam or Bun Bo Hue for patients with Type 2 Diabetes. Source: FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table 2020.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table 2020, an approved primary regional source for Southeast Asian dishes. GI value of 58 is supported by the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature for rice vermicelli. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are present and internally consistent. The per-bowl macros (510 kcal, 22g protein, 16g fat, 58g carbohydrate from 450g serving) are arithmetically consistent with the per-100g values provided.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for the assembled Bun Thit Nuong dish (including nuoc cham) is not available in the FAO/INFOODS SEA Table 2020 at the composite dish level. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the assembled dish is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per FAO/INFOODS SEA source for this dish. All core macros (protein_g 4.9, fat_g 3.6, carbohydrate_g 12.9) are non-zero and fully supported. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli (bún) GI range 58–64 from University of Sydney GI Database; the room-temperature serving and pickled vegetable acidity provide modest downward moderation. Halal status is marked false as nuoc cham contains fish sauce which may not be halal-certified depending on preparation; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant or producer.
